Utility in New South Wales, Australia

Sydney Water

**Sydney Water**, formally the **Sydney Water Corporation**, is a [New South Wales Government](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales")–[owned](/wiki/State-owned_corporation "State-owned corporation") [statutory corporation](/wiki/Statutory_authority "Statutory authority") that provides [potable](/wiki/Potable "Potable") [drinking water](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water"), [wastewater](/wiki/Wastewater "Wastewater") and some [stormwater](/wiki/Stormwater "Stormwater") services to [Greater Metropolitan Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), the [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") and the [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(New_South_Wales) "Blue Mountains (New South Wales)") regions, in the Australian state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion** with: history of water supply in Sydney since 1888. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=). *(April 2022)* |

The origins of Sydney Water go back to 26 March 1888 when the *Metropolitan Water and Sewerage Amendment Act, 1888*[[1]](#cite_note-1) was enacted and repealed certain sections of the *Sydney Corporation Act, 1879*[[2]](#cite_note-2) relating to water supply and sewerage, thereby transferring the property, powers and obligations from the [Municipal Council](/wiki/City_of_Sydney "City of Sydney") to the Board of Water Supply and Sewerage.[[3]](#cite_note-BWSS-3)

During [2023 New South Wales state election](/wiki/2023_New_South_Wales_state_election "2023 New South Wales state election"), confidential documents from [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG") and [Clayton Utz](/wiki/Clayton_Utz "Clayton Utz") regarding the [privatisation](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation") of Sydney Water were made public. [Dominic Perrottet](/wiki/Dominic_Perrottet "Dominic Perrottet"), who was the Treasurer at the time had previously declared in March 2020 that he had no plans to even do a study on privatisation, but the documents revealed that studies had taken place in January 2020 and later in November 2021, with the reporting making it clear the study was done due to direct pressure from the Government.[[4]](#cite_note-4) During and after the election NSW Labor pledged to enshrine government ownership of Sydney Water in the [NSW state constitution](/wiki/New_South_Wales_constitution "New South Wales constitution").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) a similar move that the Victorian government was doing with VEC[*[clarify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*].

### Name changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Name changes")]

The forebears of Sydney Water include:

* Board of Water Supply and Sewerage (1888–1892)[[3]](#cite_note-BWSS-3)
* Metropolitan Board of Water Supply and Sewerage (1892–1925)[[3]](#cite_note-BWSS-3)
* Metropolitan Water Sewerage and Drainage Board (1925–1987)[[7]](#cite_note-MWSDB-7)
* Water Board (1987–1994)[[7]](#cite_note-MWSDB-7) which had also been the colloquial name for the organisation for much of its history in the 20th century, and persists among longer term employees and older members of the community to this day

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Georgemaundersign.jpg/250px-Georgemaundersign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgemaundersign.jpg)

A sign authorised by Sydney Water ([Prospect Nature Reserve](/wiki/Prospect_Nature_Reserve "Prospect Nature Reserve"))

* Sydney Water Corporation Limited (1995–1999)[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* Sydney Water Corporation (1999–present) – with "Limited" being dropped when the corporation changed from a [state-owned](/wiki/State-owned "State-owned") [limited company](/wiki/Limited_company "Limited company") to a [statutory](/wiki/Statutory "Statutory") [state-owned](/wiki/State-owned "State-owned") [corporation](/wiki/Corporation "Corporation").

## Water supply management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Water supply management")]

Main article: [1998 Sydney water crisis](/wiki/1998_Sydney_water_crisis "1998 Sydney water crisis")

Sydney Water's management received extensive criticism following the [1998 Sydney water crisis](/wiki/1998_Sydney_water_crisis "1998 Sydney water crisis") regarding what was believed to be the large scale contamination of Sydney's [raw water](/wiki/Raw_water "Raw water") supply. The supposed contamination was heightened levels of [cryptosporidium](/wiki/Cryptosporidium "Cryptosporidium") and [giardia](/wiki/Giardia "Giardia") in Sydney's [Warragamba Dam](/wiki/Warragamba_Dam "Warragamba Dam"). This meant the public had to take extra steps in their own homes to ensure tap water was safe to drink.[[9]](#cite_note-9) After this event, the [Sydney Catchment Authority](/wiki/Sydney_Catchment_Authority "Sydney Catchment Authority") was created to manage Sydney's dams, [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir_(water) "Reservoir (water)"), raw water and [catchment areas](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin"). The Chairman of Sydney Water, [David Hill](/wiki/David_Hill_(businessman) "David Hill (businessman)") resigned ten days after the crisis and denied any responsibility, claiming he was leaving only to concentrate on his political career.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On 1 January 2015, The Sydney Catchment Authority was merged with State Water Corporation to form [WaterNSW](/wiki/WaterNSW "WaterNSW") so that WaterNSW is now the supplier of raw water to Sydney Water.

In June 2022, Sydney Water started working with decentralised greywater recycling system producer [Hydraloop](/w/index.php?title=Hydraloop&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hydraloop (page does not exist)") for reducing water use.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Water restrictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Water restrictions")]

Since June 2019 *Sydney Water* replaced water restrictions with *Water Wise Rules*. The *Rules* are:

Level 1 water restrictions (subject to change at any time by NSW Government)

* All hoses must now have a trigger nozzle.
* Handheld hoses, sprinklers, and watering systems may be used only before 10 am and after 4 pm on any day – to avoid the heat of the day
* No hosing of hard surfaces such as paths and driveways (spot cleaning hazards allowed). Washing vehicles is allowed.
* Fire hoses may be used for fire fighting activities only.

Commercial Businesses who use water in the course of their activities are required to apply for a permit, it's free and can be done via the internet or over the phone on 13 20 92, penalties may apply if caught working without a permit.

NSW government have empowered Sydney Water Community Water Officers the authority to impose fines of $220 for violations of the rules for individuals, $550 for businesses, and $2,200 for individuals and $4,400 for businesses [water theft](/wiki/Water_theft "Water theft"). Rules are enforced by Sydney Water staff known as Community Water Officers through random checks and intelligence gathered from the community.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Headquarters and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Headquarters and influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/MWS%26DBBuilding..png/250px-MWS%26DBBuilding..png)](/wiki/File:MWS%26DBBuilding..png)

Part of the art deco façade of the former Metropolitan Water Sewerage & Drainage Board Building

Sydney Water, and its predecessors, had for virtually all of its existence dating back to the 19th century, been located in the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") at the corner of [Pitt](/wiki/Pitt_Street "Pitt Street") and [Bathurst](/wiki/Bathurst_Street,_Sydney "Bathurst Street, Sydney") streets, directly above [Town Hall railway station](/wiki/Town_Hall_railway_station,_Sydney "Town Hall railway station, Sydney"). The central location of Sydney Water in Sydney reflected the organisation's strategic significance as the lead organisation in planning Sydney's growth and future expansion. Without water services, no residential or business growth could occur in Sydney, the Illawarra or Blue Mountains.

Sydney Water's headquarters were relocated to [Parramatta](/wiki/Parramatta "Parramatta") in May 2009.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The historic [former headquarters](/wiki/Sydney_Water_Head_Office "Sydney Water Head Office") building has been redeveloped into a hotel, with the adjacent 1969 building being extended into [a skyscraper](/wiki/Greenland_Centre_Sydney "Greenland Centre Sydney").

The developer, [Brookfield Multiplex](/wiki/Multiplex_(company) "Multiplex (company)"), valued the new Parramatta headquarters project at A$176 million.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Under this deal, rather than Sydney Water owning its own building, the corporation would enter a long-term lease with a private sector provider, who would own and maintain the corporate head office, at an annual rent charged to the NSW taxpayer.

The main suburban offices throughout Sydney were all closed in the mid-2000s, including offices at [Blacktown](/wiki/Blacktown "Blacktown"), [Rockdale](/wiki/Rockdale,_New_South_Wales "Rockdale, New South Wales"), [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool,_New_South_Wales "Liverpool, New South Wales") and [Chatswood](/wiki/Chatswood,_New_South_Wales "Chatswood, New South Wales"), with services consolidated to the Headquarters for efficiency. The Rockdale office was opened in 1990 and closed in 2004[[16]](#cite_note-16) These offices were traditionally dealing with customer queries, but saw a drop in use as customers started paying their bills via the internet[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Desalination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Desalination")]

In early 2010 operations of the [Sydney Desalination Plant](/wiki/Sydney_Desalination_Plant "Sydney Desalination Plant") began;[[18]](#cite_note-Sydney's_desal_plant_switched_on-18) with a licence granted to [Veolia Water](/wiki/Veolia_Water "Veolia Water") to operate the plant and supply Sydney Water with [drinking water](/wiki/Potable "Potable"). In 2012, the NSW Government entered into a 50–year lease with Sydney Desalination Plant Pty Ltd (DSP), a company jointly owned by the [Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan](/wiki/Ontario_Teachers%27_Pension_Plan "Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan") (50%) and two funds managed by Hastings Funds Management Limited: Utilities Trust of Australia and The Infrastructure Fund (together 50%).[[19]](#cite_note-19) The terms of the A$2.3 billion lease lock Sydney Water into a 50–year water supply agreement with DSP.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Land Ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Land Ownership")]

Sydney Water is the owner of numerous parklands and public areas in Sydney. Local councils and sporting facilities typically license or lease these properties from Sydney Water, in order to enable public use. Many sites are on land that are part of the historic [Botany Water Reserves](/wiki/Botany_Water_Reserves "Botany Water Reserves"). Properties currently owned by Sydney Water include:

* Bonnie Doon Golf Club, Pagewood
* [The Lakes Golf Club](/wiki/The_Lakes_Golf_Club "The Lakes Golf Club"), Eastlakes
* Eastlake Golf Club, Daceyville
* Mutch Park, Pagewood
* [Astrolabe Park](/wiki/Astrolabe_Park "Astrolabe Park"), Daceyville
* Linear Park, Mascot
* Gardens-R-Us (closed), Eastlakes
* The [Old Como railway bridge](/wiki/Old_Como_railway_bridge "Old Como railway bridge")
* The [Centennial Park Reservoir](/wiki/Centennial_Park_Reservoir "Centennial Park Reservoir")
* The [Woollahra Reservoir](/wiki/Woollahra_Reservoir "Woollahra Reservoir")
* The [Hyde Park Obelisk](/wiki/Hyde_Park_Obelisk "Hyde Park Obelisk")
* Gilgandra Reserve, North Bondi
* Niblick Street Reserve, North Bondi
* [Wolli Creek Regional Park](/wiki/Wolli_Creek_Regional_Park "Wolli Creek Regional Park")

## Executives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Executives")]

From 1888 to 1987, Sydney Water and its predecessors were managed by the president, who effectively served as chair of the board and managing director. However, with the passing of the *Water Board Act, 1987*, the roles were split between an executive managing director and a non-executive chairman of the board.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sydney_Water&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

Coat of arms of Sydney Water

|  |
| --- |
| Adopted  Granted to the MWSDB by the [Kings of Arms](/wiki/College_of_Arms "College of Arms"), 1 March 1965.  Crest  In front of a pick and scythe in saltire ensigned with a mural coronet Azure a wheel Argent.  Torse  A wreath of the colours (Argent and Azure).  Helm  A closed helmet.  Escutcheon  Argent gutty, a water bouget and in base two bars wavy Azure on a chief nebuly Gules an escallop Argent between a lion passant and a fleece Or.  Supporters  On the dexter side a figure representing Aquarius proper habited Azure and on the sinister side a figure representing Hygieia proper vested Argent cloaked Azure.  Compartment  A mount Vert and in base two bars wavy Azure.  Motto  [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Serviendo Sanitas* ("Serving health")  Other elements  Mantled Argent and Azure.  Badge  A water bouget Azure encircled by a Serpent devouring its tail head to the dexter Vert.  Symbolism  The wavy (nebuly) partition line represents clouds, which produce the drops of rain. The rain drops are grouped in threes to represent the Board's three major water catchments. The water bouget represents the primary responsibility of water supply and storage, and the two wavy bars underneath represent the sea and waterways to which the water is discharged via the sewerage and stormwater systems. In the top of the shield, the shell escallop is taken from the arms of Sydney's namesake, [Thomas Townshend, 1st Viscount Sydney](/wiki/Thomas_Townshend,_1st_Viscount_Sydney "Thomas Townshend, 1st Viscount Sydney"), and the gold lion and fleece are taken from the [Coat of arms of New South Wales](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_New_South_Wales "Coat of arms of New South Wales"). The crest represents the community which the Board services, with the wheel representing industry, the anchor representing the three ports of Sydney, Botany Bay, and Port Kembla, and the scythe representing agriculture. The supporters represent the functions of water supply ([Aquarius](/wiki/Aquarius_(astrology) "Aquarius (astrology)")) and sanitation ([Hygeia](/wiki/Hygeia "Hygeia")), and are derived from the arms granted to the London [Metropolitan Water Board](/wiki/Metropolitan_Water_Board "Metropolitan Water Board").[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-low-82) |
